title: Event delivery is usually at-least-once
description: Most async event sources retry on failure or ambiguity, so the same event can be delivered more than once; exactly-once is rare and costly.
body: Use when consuming queues, streams, or async invocations. These sources guarantee at-least-once delivery: on timeout, error, or visibility expiry the event is redelivered, so duplicates are normal, not exceptional. Exactly-once across distributed systems is generally unattainable without idempotent consumers. Do not write handlers that assume each event arrives once. Actions: make consumers idempotent and design side effects to tolerate replay. Validation signals include a replay test confirming single-effect behavior. Risk boundary: building on an exactly-once assumption produces double-processing bugs under normal retries — idempotency is mandatory, not optional.
